module.title: Module 5: Aircraft Loading & Performance
module.description: How a small unmanned aircraft (sUAS) flies and what limits its performance: the four forces of flight, weight & balance and center of gravity, payload trade-offs, density altitude and wind, LiPo/Li-ion battery care and hazards, and pre-flight performance planning. Covers the FAA UAG 'Loading & Performance' Area of Operation (about 7-11% of the exam).
learning_objectives: Identify the four forces of flight (lift, weight, thrust, drag) as they act on a multirotor sUAS.
Explain weight & balance and why center of gravity (CG) matters to stability and control.
Predict how added payload changes flight time, maneuverability, and battery draw.
Describe how density altitude (high, hot, humid) and wind degrade sUAS performance and endurance.
Apply safe LiPo/Li-ion battery practices for charging, storage, cold weather, and damaged packs.
Build a pre-flight performance plan using battery reserves, wind, temperature, and manufacturer data.

lessons.title: The Forces of Flight on a Small UAS
learning_objectives: Name and define lift, weight, thrust, and drag for a multirotor.
Explain how a multirotor balances these forces in hover, climb, and forward flight.
Relate added weight or drag to the power (and battery) needed to keep flying.

content.html:
Why the four forces matter to a drone pilot

Every aircraft in flight is acted on by four forces: lift, weight, thrust, and drag. A small unmanned aircraft (sUAS) is no exception. You will not be asked to do airplane aerodynamics math on the UAG exam, but you are expected to understand these forces conceptually so you can predict how loading, wind, and density altitude change what your drone can do.

On a typical multirotor (quadcopter, hexacopter), the spinning propellers act like many small rotating wings. They produce lift directly upward, and the flight controller continuously adjusts each motor's speed to balance the four forces and keep the aircraft where you command it. On a fixed-wing sUAS, the wing produces lift as air flows over it, and a separate motor/propeller produces forward thrust - much like a small airplane.

headers: What produces it on an sUAS
rows: Spinning props (multirotor) or airflow over the wing (fixed-wing)
Must at least equal weight to hold altitude; must exceed weight to climb
Gravity acting on airframe + battery + payload
Heavier = more lift/power needed = shorter flight
Propellers; on a multirotor it comes from tilting the aircraft
Needed to move and to overcome drag/wind
Air resistance against the airframe, props, and payload
More drag (wind, bulky payload) = more thrust/power needed

content.html:
How a multirotor uses the forces

In a steady hover, lift exactly equals weight and the aircraft is motionless. To climb, the flight controller spins the props faster so lift exceeds weight. To move horizontally, a multirotor tilts in the direction of travel; part of the props' thrust now pushes the aircraft sideways while the rest still supports its weight. That is why a heavily tilted, fast-moving drone in wind works its motors hard - it is fighting drag and supporting weight at the same time, which drains the battery faster.

Understanding this gives you a simple mental model: anything that adds weight or drag forces the motors to work harder, drawing more current and cutting your flight time. Payload, wind, and thin (low-density) air all do exactly that.

content.alt: Diagram of the four forces of flight on a quadcopter
content.caption: Figure: The four forces of flight on a multirotor sUAS - lift up, weight down, thrust in the direction of travel, and drag opposing it. Show the aircraft tilted forward in cruise.

content.text: Memory hook: Lift and Thrust are the 'engine' forces you generate; Weight and Drag are the 'penalty' forces you must overcome. Every penalty you add (payload, wind, thin air) costs battery.

content.title: Key Takeaways
items: The four forces of flight are lift, weight, thrust, and drag.
Lift opposes weight; thrust opposes drag.
A multirotor hovers when lift equals weight and climbs when lift exceeds weight.
A multirotor moves by tilting, so cruising in wind makes the motors fight drag and weight together.
Anything that adds weight or drag increases the power the motors draw and shortens flight time.

flashcards.definition: The upward force that opposes weight; on a multirotor it is produced by the spinning propellers, on a fixed-wing by airflow over the wing.
flashcards.hint_1: Opposes weight; must exceed it to climb.

flashcards.definition: The downward force of gravity acting on the total mass of the aircraft, battery, and payload.
The force that moves the aircraft in the direction of travel; on a multirotor it comes from tilting the aircraft so the props push it forward.
Air resistance that opposes the aircraft's motion; increased by wind, bulky payloads, and fast flight.

images_needed.caption: Quadcopter in forward cruise with the four forces labeled: lift (up), weight (down), thrust (forward), drag (rearward), aircraft tilted into the direction of travel.

lessons.title: Weight, Balance, and Center of Gravity
learning_objectives: Define center of gravity (CG) for an sUAS.
Explain the effects of a forward vs. aft (and lateral) CG on stability and control.
Apply manufacturer maximum takeoff weight and CG limits when mounting payloads.

content.html:
What 'weight & balance' means for a drone

Two numbers govern how a drone is loaded: how much it weighs and where that weight sits. Total weight is the airframe plus battery plus payload plus any attachments, and it must stay at or below the manufacturer's maximum takeoff weight. Balance is about distribution - whether the weight is centered so the aircraft hangs level.

The center of gravity (CG) is the single point at which the aircraft's entire weight can be considered to act - the point about which it would balance if you could suspend it. A multirotor is designed to fly with its CG at (or very near) the geometric center, directly under and between the rotors. When you add a camera, sensor, or delivery payload, you move the CG. Keeping the CG within the manufacturer's limits keeps the aircraft stable and controllable; pushing it out of limits makes the aircraft fight you.

content.alt: Diagram showing center of gravity on a quadcopter, centered vs shifted
content.caption: Figure: CG location on a multirotor. Show (a) a balanced drone with CG at the geometric center, and (b) the CG shifted forward/to one side by an off-center payload, with the aircraft tilting to compensate.

content.html:
Effects of a forward, aft, or off-center CG

On a multirotor, an off-center CG forces the flight controller to run some motors continuously harder than others just to hold a level hover. That has real consequences:

  • Forward CG (payload mounted too far forward): the nose wants to drop. The aircraft becomes more 'nose-heavy' and stable in pitch but sluggish and less responsive; the rear motors work harder, draining the battery unevenly and shortening flight time.
  • Aft CG (weight too far rearward): the aircraft becomes less stable and twitchy, harder to control smoothly, and more prone to oscillation or loss of control. An excessively aft CG is the more dangerous condition because it erodes stability.
  • Lateral (side-to-side) imbalance: the drone drifts or leans toward the heavy side; motors on the heavy side run hotter and that battery sags faster.

The general aviation rule of thumb carries over: a forward CG increases stability but reduces maneuverability and efficiency, while an aft CG reduces stability and can make the aircraft unmanageable.

rows: Responsive, even motor load, full rated flight time
Sluggish, less maneuverable, uneven battery drain
Hard to control; risk of loss of control
Heavy-side motors overworked; uneven drain, overheating

content.text: An out-of-balance or overloaded sUAS is unstable, hard to control, less efficient, and more likely to suffer a loss of control. Always mount payloads so the CG stays centered and within the manufacturer's published limits.

sections.title: How to keep the CG where it belongs
sections.content:
• Mount on the centerline. Place cameras, gimbals, and sensors on the aircraft's designed mounting point so the load sits under the geometric center.
  • Balance opposing loads. If you add weight on one side, offset it (or use the manufacturer's balancing provisions) so the aircraft hangs level.
  • Secure everything. A payload that shifts in flight moves the CG mid-flight - secure all loads so nothing slides, swings, or detaches.
  • Re-check after changes. Swapping a heavier battery or a different camera changes both total weight and CG; verify a stable, level hover at low altitude before climbing.

content.title: Key Takeaways
items: Total weight (airframe + battery + payload + attachments) must stay at or below the manufacturer's maximum takeoff weight.
The center of gravity (CG) is the point about which the aircraft's weight balances; it must stay within manufacturer limits.
A forward CG increases stability but reduces maneuverability and efficiency.
An aft CG reduces stability and can make the aircraft twitchy or uncontrollable.
Mount payloads on the centerline and secure them so the CG stays centered and cannot shift in flight.

flashcards.definition: The single point at which the entire weight of the aircraft is considered to act - the balance point. It must remain within the manufacturer's limits.
flashcards.hint_1: The point the aircraft would balance on.

flashcards.definition: The manufacturer's published limit for total weight (airframe + battery + payload + attachments) at which the aircraft may legally and safely take off.
Increases stability but makes the aircraft nose-heavy, sluggish, less maneuverable, and less efficient.
Reduces stability; the aircraft becomes twitchy, prone to oscillation, and can become uncontrollable.

images_needed.caption: Side-by-side CG diagram: balanced multirotor with CG at center vs. one with an off-center payload shifting the CG, showing the aircraft tilting and motors compensating.

lessons.title: Payload Effects: Flight Time, Maneuverability, and Battery Draw
learning_objectives: Explain why heavier payloads require more lift, more power, and yield shorter flight time.
Describe how payload affects maneuverability, climb, and reserve.
Plan payload so the aircraft stays within weight, CG, and endurance margins.

content.html:
The payload trade-off

Every gram you add to a drone has to be lifted. To support a heavier total weight, the props must produce more lift, which requires the motors to spin faster and draw more current from the battery. The direct result is shorter flight time and a smaller safety reserve. Adding payload is always a trade-off: more capability (a bigger camera, a sensor, a delivery package) in exchange for less endurance and less margin.

Heavier loading also degrades performance across the board. An overloaded drone climbs more slowly, is less maneuverable (it accelerates and stops more sluggishly), and has a higher risk of loss of control, especially in gusty wind or when a low battery can no longer deliver the current the motors demand. In thin air (high density altitude), these effects compound - which is why pilots often reduce payload on hot, high days.

content.text: Heavier payload means more lift required, more power drawn, and a shorter flight. Never load to the point that you cannot land with a safe battery reserve, and never exceed the maximum takeoff weight.

content.html:
Asymmetric loads and shifting cargo

Payload affects not just how much the aircraft carries but where. An asymmetric payload (heavier on one side or end) shifts the CG and forces some motors to overwork, which both worsens control and drains those cells faster. Worse, a load that is not secured can shift during flight - changing the CG in the air, sometimes suddenly, and potentially causing a loss of control. Secure every payload, balance it about the center, and confirm a clean, level hover before you commit to the mission.

sections.title: Worked example: payload vs. flight time
sections.content:
Suppose a manufacturer rates a quadcopter for 30 minutes of hover with no payload and 18 minutes with a 1.0 lb payload. You plan a job needing a 0.5 lb camera in moderate wind on a warm day. From the data you should expect:

  • Flight time noticeably shorter than 30 min and likely less than the 18 min figure once you add wind and warm (lower-density) air, which the chart's standard-condition numbers do not include.
  • So you plan conservatively - assume well under 18 minutes of usable time, and land with a reserve (commonly land by 20-30% battery). If the math is tight, fly multiple shorter sorties or use a larger/spare battery instead of stretching one flight.

The lesson: manufacturer numbers are best-case, standard-condition values. Real payload, wind, and temperature always make endurance worse - never better.

content.title: Key Takeaways
items: Heavier payload -> more lift required -> more power -> shorter flight time and smaller reserve.
Overloading reduces climb rate and maneuverability and raises the risk of loss of control.
Asymmetric or unsecured payloads shift the CG and can cause loss of control; secure and balance every load.
Manufacturer flight times are best-case, standard-condition values - real conditions only make endurance worse.
Plan payload so you always land with a safe battery reserve and never exceed maximum takeoff weight.

flashcards.definition: Adding payload increases the lift and power required, which increases battery draw and shortens flight time and reserve.
Reduced climb performance, reduced maneuverability, shorter flight time, smaller reserve, and increased risk of stall/loss of control.
A load mounted off-center that shifts the CG, overworks some motors, and can cause loss of control - especially if it shifts in flight.

images_needed.caption: Simple line chart: flight time (minutes) decreasing as payload weight increases, with a note that wind and high density altitude push the curve lower.

lessons.title: Density Altitude and Wind: Performance and Endurance
learning_objectives: Define density altitude and the 'high, hot, humid' conditions that raise it.
Explain why high density altitude reduces lift, thrust, and propeller efficiency.
Describe how wind, gusts, and shear degrade control and endurance.

content.html:
Density altitude - the air the drone 'feels'

Density altitude is pressure altitude corrected for non-standard temperature - essentially, the altitude at which the air density makes the aircraft feel like it is flying. Standard sea-level conditions are 29.92 inHg and 15°C. When the air is less dense, the aircraft performs as if it were higher than it really is, and performance degrades.

Three things make air less dense and therefore raise density altitude: high elevation, high temperature, and high humidity - the classic memory aid 'High, Hot, and Humid.' Thinner air means the propellers have fewer air molecules to push against, so they produce less lift and less thrust and become less efficient. The motors must spin faster to compensate, drawing more current. The practical results on a hot day at high elevation: slower climb, reduced payload capacity, sluggish handling, and shorter flight time.

content.text: High, Hot, and Humid all RAISE density altitude and HURT performance. On such days, expect slower climb, less payload, and shorter flight time - reduce your load and plan conservatively.

content.html:
Wind, gusts, and wind shear

Small, light aircraft are very wind-sensitive. Holding position or flying a path in wind forces the drone to tilt and run its motors harder to fight the wind, which drains the battery faster and shortens endurance. Flying upwind (into the wind) costs extra power; flying downwind increases groundspeed and can carry the aircraft farther than intended. Gusts and wind shear (sudden changes in wind speed or direction) degrade control and can momentarily overwhelm the flight controller.

Watch especially for mechanical turbulence - the swirling, gusty air created when wind flows around buildings, trees, and terrain near your launch site. A microburst from a nearby thunderstorm produces extreme, rapidly shifting wind shear that can slam a small UA to the ground; avoid thunderstorms entirely. A good operational habit: plan your return so you are flying back into the wind low on battery, not discovering you cannot make it home against a headwind.

content.text: Fighting wind drains the battery faster and shortens endurance. Plan to return into the wind, watch for mechanical turbulence near buildings and terrain, and never fly into or near thunderstorms (microburst wind shear).

content.title: Key Takeaways
items: Density altitude is pressure altitude corrected for non-standard temperature - the altitude the aircraft 'feels.'
High, Hot, and Humid all raise density altitude and reduce lift, thrust, and prop efficiency.
High density altitude means slower climb, reduced payload, sluggish handling, and shorter flight time - reduce payload.
Wind makes the motors work harder and drains the battery faster, shortening endurance.
Watch for gusts, wind shear, and mechanical turbulence near obstacles; avoid thunderstorms and microbursts.

flashcards.definition: Pressure altitude corrected for non-standard temperature; the altitude at which the air density makes the aircraft perform as if it were flying. High density altitude degrades performance.
The three conditions (high elevation, high temperature, high humidity) that lower air density, raise density altitude, and reduce sUAS lift, thrust, and efficiency.

flashcards.term: Effect of high density altitude on sUAS
flashcards.definition: Thinner air gives the props less to push against, so there is less lift and thrust: slower climb, reduced payload, sluggish handling, and shorter flight time.
Swirling, gusty air created when wind flows around buildings, trees, or terrain - a control hazard for sUAS near the launch site.

images_needed.caption: Illustration of 'High, Hot, Humid' raising density altitude: a drone climbing sluggishly on a hot day at high elevation vs. a brisk climb on a cool, low day.

lessons.title: Battery Care, Hazards, and Pre-Flight Performance Planning
learning_objectives: Apply safe charging, storage, and handling practices for LiPo/Li-ion packs.
Recognize battery hazards: thermal runaway, swelling, damage, and cold-weather effects.
Build a pre-flight performance plan and read manufacturer performance data.

content.html:
LiPo and Li-ion batteries: power and hazard

Most sUAS are powered by lithium polymer (LiPo) or lithium-ion (Li-ion) battery packs. They are prized for being energy-dense - a lot of stored energy in a light package - but that same energy density makes them hazardous if mistreated. The principal danger is thermal runaway: an internal short or overheating triggers a self-feeding chemical reaction that rapidly heats the cell, leading to fire or explosion. Thermal runaway can be caused by overcharging, over-discharging, short-circuiting, puncturing, or physically damaging a pack.

A LiPo fire is intense and hard to extinguish, and a damaged pack can ignite without warning - sometimes hours later. That is why battery handling is a core safety skill, not an afterthought. The single most important rule: never fly or charge a damaged or swollen pack.

content.alt: Photo of a healthy LiPo pack next to a swollen (puffed) LiPo pack
content.caption: Figure: A healthy LiPo pack (flat, firm) next to a swollen/puffed pack. A swollen pack must be retired immediately and never charged or flown.

rows: Use the correct LiPo/Li-ion charger; never leave charging unattended; let hot packs cool before charging
Wrong charger or overcharge can trigger thermal runaway
Store at storage charge (~3.8 V/cell, ~50%), cool and dry, in a fireproof bag/container
Full or empty long-term storage stresses cells and shortens life
Never fly or charge a swollen, puffed, punctured, or damaged pack - retire it
Respect the low-voltage cutoff; do not over-discharge or fly to 0%
Over-discharge damages cells and risks sudden voltage sag/loss of power
Keep packs warm before flight; expect reduced capacity and shorter flights
Cold lowers capacity and voltage, risking sudden power loss
Have a fireproof storage/transport method and a plan to smother/cool a fire
LiPo fires are intense and re-ignite

content.text: Never fly or charge a damaged or swollen (puffed) LiPo pack. Swelling means the cell is failing - retire it immediately. Never leave a charging pack unattended, and store packs at storage charge in a fireproof container.

content.html:
Cold weather and battery reserves

Cold is a quiet performance killer. Low temperatures reduce a LiPo's capacity and voltage, so a battery that gives 20 minutes in mild weather may give far less in the cold - and can suffer a sudden voltage drop under load that forces an unexpected landing. In winter, pre-warm packs, expect shorter flights, and land earlier. Year-round, the rule is the same: always plan with a battery reserve and never fly a pack down to 0%. Many operators set a hard 'return-to-home' or land trigger at 20-30% remaining so an unexpected headwind or sag still leaves margin to land safely.

Reading manufacturer performance data

Manufacturers publish performance specifications - maximum flight time, hover time, maximum payload, maximum wind resistance, and operating temperature range - and sometimes a performance chart (for example, flight time vs. payload, or vs. temperature). These figures are best-case, standard-condition numbers: a 'max flight time' is usually measured in still air, at a steady speed, with no payload, on a fresh battery in mild temperatures. Your real flight - with payload, wind, maneuvering, and non-standard temperature - will always be shorter. Use the published data as a starting point, then discount it for your actual conditions and subtract your reserve. When a chart gives values at intervals (e.g., 0, 0.5, 1.0 lb payload), interpolate conservatively and round in the safe direction.

sections.title: Pre-flight performance planning checklist
sections.content:
Before each operation, plan performance the way 14 CFR 107.49 expects you to assess the operating environment:

  • Battery: packs fully charged, healthy (no swelling/damage), warm if cold out; plan a reserve and a hard land/RTH trigger (e.g., 20-30%).
  • Wind: check current and forecast wind, gusts, and direction; confirm they are within the aircraft's wind-resistance spec; plan to return into the wind.
  • Temperature & density altitude: hot/high/humid? Expect degraded performance and reduce payload.
  • Weight & balance: total weight at or below max takeoff weight; CG centered; payload secured.
  • Site assessment: obstacles, terrain, and buildings that create mechanical turbulence; people and property; a clear takeoff/landing area and an emergency landing spot over the least-populated ground.
  • Manufacturer data: review flight-time/payload/wind specs and discount them for today's conditions.

content.text: Good performance planning = healthy charged batteries + a reserve, wind within limits and a return-into-wind plan, payload within weight/CG limits, density-altitude awareness, and a site assessment - all discounted from best-case manufacturer numbers.

content.title: Key Takeaways
items: LiPo/Li-ion packs are energy-dense but hazardous; thermal runaway can cause fire or explosion.
Never fly or charge a damaged or swollen pack; charge with the correct charger and never leave it unattended.
Store packs at storage charge (~3.8 V/cell, ~50%), cool, dry, and in a fireproof container.
Cold reduces battery capacity and voltage and can cause sudden power loss - pre-warm packs and expect shorter flights.
Always plan a battery reserve (commonly land by 20-30%) and never fly a pack to 0%.
Manufacturer performance data are best-case, standard-condition values - discount them for payload, wind, and temperature.

flashcards.definition: A self-feeding chemical reaction in a lithium battery cell that rapidly generates heat, leading to fire or explosion; triggered by overcharge, over-discharge, short circuit, puncture, or damage.
flashcards.hint_1: The runaway heating that causes LiPo fires.

flashcards.definition: A battery pack that has visibly bulged, indicating internal failure. It must be retired immediately and never charged or flown.
The recommended long-term storage state for a LiPo, about 3.8 V per cell (~50% capacity), kept cool, dry, and in a fireproof container.
Cold reduces a LiPo's capacity and voltage, shortening flight time and risking a sudden voltage drop and unexpected landing.
Charge intentionally kept in reserve so the aircraft can always land safely; pilots commonly set a hard land/RTH trigger at 20-30% and never fly to 0%.

images_needed.caption: Comparison photo of a flat, healthy LiPo pack vs. a swollen/puffed pack, with a caption noting that swollen packs must be retired.
Sample manufacturer performance table/chart: flight time vs. payload and vs. temperature, illustrating how to read and discount best-case figures.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which two of the four forces of flight are the 'penalty' forces that an sUAS must overcome to fly?
options: Weight and drag
Lift and thrust
Lift and weight
quiz_questions.correct_answer: Weight and drag
quiz_questions.explanation: Lift and thrust are the forces the aircraft generates (the 'engine' forces); weight and drag are the opposing 'penalty' forces it must overcome. Lift opposes weight and thrust opposes drag.

quiz_questions.question_text: On a multirotor sUAS holding a steady, motionless hover, what is the relationship between lift and weight?
options: Lift exactly equals weight
Lift exceeds weight
Lift is less than weight
quiz_questions.correct_answer: Lift exactly equals weight
quiz_questions.explanation: In a steady hover the aircraft is neither climbing nor descending, so lift exactly equals weight. To climb, lift must exceed weight; if lift is less than weight, the aircraft descends.

quiz_questions.question_text: How does a multirotor produce thrust to move horizontally?
options: It tilts in the direction of travel so part of the props' force pushes it forward
It uses a separate forward-facing propeller
It increases lift on all motors equally
quiz_questions.correct_answer: It tilts in the direction of travel so part of the props' force pushes it forward
quiz_questions.explanation: A multirotor has no separate forward propeller; it tilts so a component of the rotors' thrust pushes it horizontally while the rest still supports its weight. Increasing all motors equally would only climb.

quiz_questions.question_text: Why does cruising into a strong wind drain a multirotor's battery faster?
options: The motors must work harder to overcome drag while still supporting the aircraft's weight
The wind cools the motors, increasing their resistance
Lift is no longer required in forward flight
quiz_questions.correct_answer: The motors must work harder to overcome drag while still supporting the aircraft's weight
quiz_questions.explanation: Flying into wind increases drag, so the motors must produce extra thrust to overcome it while still supporting the weight - drawing more current. Lift is still required in forward flight, and wind cooling does not increase resistance.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does the term 'center of gravity' (CG) refer to on an sUAS?
options: The point at which the aircraft's entire weight is considered to act
The geometric center of the battery
The point of maximum lift on the propellers
quiz_questions.correct_answer: The point at which the aircraft's entire weight is considered to act
quiz_questions.explanation: The CG is the single balance point at which the aircraft's total weight is considered to act. It must stay within the manufacturer's limits for safe, stable, controllable flight.

quiz_questions.question_text: An sUAS is loaded so its center of gravity is too far aft. What is the most likely effect?
options: Reduced stability, making the aircraft twitchy and harder to control
Increased stability and longer flight time
No effect as long as total weight is within limits
quiz_questions.correct_answer: Reduced stability, making the aircraft twitchy and harder to control
quiz_questions.explanation: An aft CG reduces stability and can make the aircraft twitchy, prone to oscillation, and potentially uncontrollable. CG matters even when total weight is within limits, because balance affects controllability.

quiz_questions.question_text: Compared with a balanced load, what does a forward CG generally do to an sUAS?
options: Increases stability but reduces maneuverability and efficiency
Increases both stability and maneuverability
Reduces stability and increases responsiveness
quiz_questions.correct_answer: Increases stability but reduces maneuverability and efficiency
quiz_questions.explanation: A forward CG makes the aircraft more stable (nose-heavy) but sluggish and less maneuverable, and it drains the battery unevenly. An aft CG, by contrast, reduces stability and increases twitchiness.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which statement about total weight on a Part 107 sUAS is correct?
options: Total weight (airframe + battery + payload + attachments) must stay at or below the manufacturer's maximum takeoff weight
Only the payload counts toward the weight limit
Weight limits only matter at high density altitude
quiz_questions.correct_answer: Total weight (airframe + battery + payload + attachments) must stay at or below the manufacturer's maximum takeoff weight
quiz_questions.explanation: Total weight includes the airframe, battery, payload, and any attachments, and it must remain at or below the manufacturer's maximum takeoff weight. Weight always matters, though high density altitude makes overloading worse.

quiz_questions.question_text: Why is it important to securely mount and balance a payload before flight?
options: An unsecured load can shift in flight, moving the CG and causing a loss of control
Securing the payload increases the maximum takeoff weight
Balancing the payload eliminates the need to check the battery
quiz_questions.correct_answer: An unsecured load can shift in flight, moving the CG and causing a loss of control
quiz_questions.explanation: A load that shifts in flight changes the CG in the air, possibly suddenly, and can cause loss of control. Securing a load does not change the weight limit or remove other pre-flight checks.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is the primary effect of adding payload to an sUAS?
options: More lift and power are required, shortening flight time
Flight time increases because the aircraft is more stable
The aircraft climbs faster
quiz_questions.correct_answer: More lift and power are required, shortening flight time
quiz_questions.explanation: Heavier payload requires more lift, so the motors draw more current and flight time and reserve drop. Added weight also slows the climb and reduces maneuverability rather than improving them.

quiz_questions.question_text: An overloaded sUAS will most likely experience which of the following?
options: Reduced climb rate, reduced maneuverability, and a higher risk of loss of control
Improved endurance and faster climb
No change as long as the CG is centered
quiz_questions.correct_answer: Reduced climb rate, reduced maneuverability, and a higher risk of loss of control
quiz_questions.explanation: Overloading degrades climb and maneuverability and increases the risk of loss of control, in addition to shortening flight time. A centered CG does not offset the penalties of excess total weight.

quiz_questions.question_text: A manufacturer lists a maximum flight time of 28 minutes. How should a remote pilot treat this figure when planning a job that carries a camera payload in moderate wind on a warm day?
options: As a best-case value to be discounted, expecting significantly less usable time
As a guaranteed minimum flight time under any conditions
As the time available after the battery reaches 0%
quiz_questions.correct_answer: As a best-case value to be discounted, expecting significantly less usable time
quiz_questions.explanation: Published flight times are best-case, standard-condition values (no payload, still air, mild temperature, fresh battery). Real payload, wind, and warm air all shorten endurance, so the pilot should plan for significantly less and keep a reserve.

quiz_questions.question_text: How does an asymmetric (off-center) payload affect an sUAS?
options: It shifts the CG and forces some motors to overwork, worsening control and draining those cells faster
It has no effect as long as total weight is under the limit
It improves stability by lowering the CG
quiz_questions.correct_answer: It shifts the CG and forces some motors to overwork, worsening control and draining those cells faster
quiz_questions.explanation: An off-center load moves the CG, so the flight controller must run some motors harder to stay level, which worsens control and drains the battery unevenly. Total weight being within limits does not excuse a CG that is out of balance.

quiz_questions.question_text: Density altitude is best described as:
options: Pressure altitude corrected for non-standard temperature
The aircraft's height above ground level
The altitude indicated on the controller's GPS
quiz_questions.correct_answer: Pressure altitude corrected for non-standard temperature
quiz_questions.explanation: Density altitude is pressure altitude corrected for non-standard temperature - the altitude at which the air density makes the aircraft 'feel' like it is flying. It is not a height above ground or a GPS readout.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which combination of conditions produces a HIGH density altitude and the WORST sUAS performance?
options: High elevation, high temperature, and high humidity
Low elevation, cool temperature, and dry air
Sea level, standard temperature, and high pressure
quiz_questions.correct_answer: High elevation, high temperature, and high humidity
quiz_questions.explanation: 'High, Hot, and Humid' all lower air density and raise density altitude, reducing lift, thrust, and propeller efficiency. Cool, dry, low, high-pressure conditions give the best performance.

quiz_questions.question_text: On a hot day at a high-elevation site, what should a remote pilot expect from the aircraft's performance?
options: Slower climb, reduced payload capacity, and shorter flight time
Faster climb and longer flight time
No change because density altitude only affects manned aircraft
quiz_questions.correct_answer: Slower climb, reduced payload capacity, and shorter flight time
quiz_questions.explanation: High density altitude means thinner air, so the props produce less lift and thrust: the drone climbs more slowly, carries less, and flies shorter. Density altitude affects small UAS just as it affects manned aircraft.

quiz_questions.question_text: Why should a remote pilot plan to fly back toward the launch point INTO the wind while the battery is getting low?
options: Flying into a headwind takes extra power, so it should be done while reserve remains rather than discovering you cannot make it home
A headwind charges the battery during the return
Wind has no effect on a returning aircraft
quiz_questions.correct_answer: Flying into a headwind takes extra power, so it should be done while reserve remains rather than discovering you cannot make it home
quiz_questions.explanation: Returning into the wind costs extra power; doing it while you still have reserve avoids being stranded downwind on a dying battery. Wind does not charge the battery, and it certainly affects a returning aircraft.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is 'mechanical turbulence' and why does it concern an sUAS pilot?
options: Swirling, gusty air created when wind flows around buildings or terrain - a control hazard near the launch site
Vibration caused by a worn motor bearing
Turbulence felt only above 400 ft AGL
quiz_questions.correct_answer: Swirling, gusty air created when wind flows around buildings or terrain - a control hazard near the launch site
quiz_questions.explanation: Mechanical turbulence is the chaotic air produced when wind tumbles around obstacles such as buildings, trees, and terrain. It can degrade control right where the drone operates, near the ground and structures.

quiz_questions.question_text: How does wind affect an sUAS battery's endurance?
options: Fighting wind makes the motors work harder, draining the battery faster and shortening endurance
Wind extends endurance by providing free lift
Wind has no measurable effect on endurance
quiz_questions.correct_answer: Fighting wind makes the motors work harder, draining the battery faster and shortening endurance
quiz_questions.explanation: Holding position or flying a path in wind forces the aircraft to tilt and work its motors harder to overcome drag, increasing current draw and shortening endurance. Light aircraft are especially wind-sensitive.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is the principal hazard of LiPo/Li-ion batteries that makes careful handling essential?
options: Thermal runaway leading to fire or explosion
Gradual loss of color over time
They become heavier as they discharge
quiz_questions.correct_answer: Thermal runaway leading to fire or explosion
quiz_questions.explanation: The chief danger is thermal runaway - a self-feeding heating reaction that can cause fire or explosion, triggered by overcharge, over-discharge, short circuit, puncture, or damage. Batteries do not change weight as they discharge.

quiz_questions.question_text: You notice that a LiPo pack has become swollen (puffed up). What should you do?
options: Retire it immediately and do not charge or fly it
Charge it slowly to flatten it back out
Use it for one more flight, then discard it
quiz_questions.correct_answer: Retire it immediately and do not charge or fly it
quiz_questions.explanation: Swelling indicates internal cell failure; a puffed pack must be retired immediately and never charged or flown, because it can ignite spontaneously. There is no safe way to 'flatten' or use a swollen pack.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which is the recommended way to store a LiPo battery for the long term?
options: At storage charge (about 3.8 V/cell, ~50%), cool, dry, and in a fireproof container
Fully charged so it is always ready to fly
Fully discharged to zero to prevent fire
quiz_questions.correct_answer: At storage charge (about 3.8 V/cell, ~50%), cool, dry, and in a fireproof container
quiz_questions.explanation: LiPos should be stored at about 50% (storage charge, ~3.8 V/cell), cool, dry, and in a fireproof bag or container. Storing fully charged or fully discharged for long periods stresses the cells and shortens their life.

quiz_questions.question_text: How does cold weather typically affect a LiPo-powered sUAS?
options: Reduced battery capacity and voltage, leading to shorter flight time and possible sudden power loss
Increased capacity and longer flights
No effect on the battery, only on the pilot's comfort
quiz_questions.correct_answer: Reduced battery capacity and voltage, leading to shorter flight time and possible sudden power loss
quiz_questions.explanation: Cold reduces a LiPo's capacity and voltage, shortening flight time and risking a sudden voltage sag under load that can force an unexpected landing. Pre-warm packs and expect shorter flights in cold weather.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which charging practice helps prevent a battery fire?
options: Use the correct LiPo charger and never leave a charging pack unattended
Charge with any available USB charger to save time
Charge a hot pack immediately after landing to keep it warm
quiz_questions.correct_answer: Use the correct LiPo charger and never leave a charging pack unattended
quiz_questions.explanation: Always charge with the proper LiPo/Li-ion charger and stay with the pack while it charges so you can react if it overheats. The wrong charger can overcharge a pack, and a hot pack should be allowed to cool before charging.

quiz_questions.question_text: Why should a remote pilot always plan a battery reserve and avoid flying a pack to 0%?
options: A reserve leaves margin to land safely if wind, payload, or voltage sag reduce the expected flight time
Flying to 0% extends the battery's life
Reserves are only required at night
quiz_questions.correct_answer: A reserve leaves margin to land safely if wind, payload, or voltage sag reduce the expected flight time
quiz_questions.explanation: A battery reserve (many pilots set a hard land/RTH trigger around 20-30%) ensures you can still land if conditions cut your endurance or the voltage sags. Over-discharging to 0% damages the cells and is not a day/night issue.

quiz_questions.question_text: When using a manufacturer's published 'maximum flight time,' how should it be interpreted?
options: As a best-case, standard-condition value that will be shorter with payload, wind, and non-standard temperature
As the guaranteed flight time in any weather with full payload
As the time available only after the reserve is used up
quiz_questions.correct_answer: As a best-case, standard-condition value that will be shorter with payload, wind, and non-standard temperature
quiz_questions.explanation: Maximum flight time is measured under ideal, standard conditions (no payload, still air, mild temperature). Real operations with payload, wind, maneuvering, and non-standard temperatures will always yield shorter times, so discount the figure and keep a reserve.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which item belongs in a pre-flight performance plan for an sUAS operation?
options: Confirming wind is within the aircraft's limits and planning to return into the wind
Assuming the manufacturer's max flight time is achievable today
Flying the battery to 0% to maximize coverage
quiz_questions.correct_answer: Confirming wind is within the aircraft's limits and planning to return into the wind
quiz_questions.explanation: Sound performance planning checks wind against the aircraft's wind-resistance limits and plans a return into the wind, in addition to weight/CG, density altitude, battery reserves, and a site assessment. You should not assume best-case flight time or fly to 0%.
